event_name: Symbols of the Cemetery Walking Tour
blurb:
This one sounds unusual. It absolutely is, and that's exactly why it's worth going.

The Kennesaw Cemetery Preservation Foundation leads a guided walking tour through the historic city cemetery, unpacking the symbolism carved into gravestones throughout the grounds. Lambs, anchors, clasped hands, broken columns: every symbol meant something specific to the people who chose it, and most of us walk past them without a second thought. This tour changes that.

It's 90 minutes on a Sunday evening, it's free, and it's the kind of local history experience that sticks with you. Bring the curious teenager who thinks history class is boring. They'll leave with different questions than they came in with.
scoring_notes: Unique local history event with strong educational angle. Good fit for the educated East Cobb audience (65% bachelor's degree+). Free and family-appropriate. The Father's Day Sunday timing is a slight headwind, but evening hours (6 PM) sidestep the traditional brunch/lunch window. Scored well on uniqueness because this type of event is genuinely rare.

blurb:
Cigar people already know BURN at The Battery. If you've been curious and haven't made it in yet, this Thursday is a good excuse.

Plasencia is doing a special event featuring exclusive deals on their lineup, specialty drinks, and the last remaining stock of their limited-edition Year of the Horse. That's not marketing language: once those are gone, they're gone. If you've been eyeing a bottle of something rare, this is the room to be in.

It runs Thursday from 7 to 11 PM. Good cigars, good drinks, a crowd that actually knows what they're talking about. For the right person in East Cobb, this is a GREAT Thursday night.
scoring_notes: Cigar events are niche but have strong appeal in affluent suburban demographics like East Cobb. The limited-edition Plasencia Year of the Horse angle adds genuine scarcity and uniqueness. Adult skew works fine for this audience. BURN at The Battery is well-known regionally. Docked slightly on audience match because cigar culture is a subset of the overall readership.

blurb:
The Battery throws a block party before select home games, and if you haven't been to one yet, you've been leaving a good Saturday afternoon on the table.

From noon to 4 PM on Saturday, The Battery turns into a full pre-game scene: live entertainment, energy, food everywhere, and a crowd that's already locked in before the first pitch. You don't need a game ticket to come hang out, which means this works just as well as a standalone afternoon as it does as a warmup for the stands.

East Cobb to The Battery is a short drive. Go early, grab lunch, let the kids soak it in. It's one of those summer afternoons that doesn't cost much but feels like it did.
scoring_notes: Block parties are a recurring Battery format, which docks the uniqueness score. But The Battery is essentially East Cobb's entertainment hub, and this audience has strong Braves affinity. Good family play and genuinely free. Works well as a featured event in a week that includes multiple Battery-area events.

venue: The Art Place at Mountain View, Marietta
blurb:
Outdoor movie night is back at The Art Place at Mountain View, and this one's worth showing up for early.

Scene on the Green is screening Superman (2025) under the stars on Saturday night. It's free, it's right in your backyard, and it's the new Superman, so the kids who've been asking about it since it came out finally have their answer. Bring a blanket, bring chairs, bring snacks. The show starts at 7:30 PM but the night starts whenever you get there.

For a free Saturday night out that doesn't feel like a compromise, this one delivers. Just don't be the family that shows up at 7:29 and can't find a good spot.
scoring_notes: Free outdoor movies are a recurring format, but screening a brand-new blockbuster keeps the uniqueness score up. East Cobb families with teens are the sweet spot here. Very close to home, zero cost, and a recognizable film. Strong community engagement play for the newsletter.

blurb:
If you know wine, you know Screaming Eagle. Jonata comes from the same ownership, same Santa Barbara County terroir, and the same obsessive approach to what ends up in the bottle. This Tuesday, there's an intimate five-course dinner built around their lineup, and "intimate" is the operative word here.

These aren't big room events. They're the kind of dinner where the pours are generous, the conversation is good, and you actually learn something about what you're drinking. Five courses, carefully matched wines, a Tuesday night that punches well above its weight.

At $118K household income, East Cobb is exactly the audience Jonata is made for. If wine is your thing, this is worth clearing your Tuesday for. Seats will be limited.
scoring_notes: High-end wine dinners featuring estate-level California producers are genuinely rare and one-night-only events. Strong fit for empty nesters and established couples in this income bracket. Sandy Springs is a reasonable drive from East Cobb. Uniqueness score is high because Jonata/Screaming Eagle ownership is a serious wine credential. Audience match docked slightly because it's adult-only and niche.

event_name: FIFA World Cup Watch Party: Argentina vs. Austria
venue: The Plaza at Battery Atlanta, Atlanta
blurb:
The World Cup is on, and The Battery is turning the Plaza Green into one of the better places in Atlanta to watch it.

Argentina vs. Austria goes up on the big outdoor screen Monday afternoon. Messi. Lawn chairs. Twenty-plus restaurants within walking distance. You grab your food, you find your spot, and your drink can roam with you anywhere on The Battery grounds. It's a genuinely good setup for a match of this size.

If your household has been watching the tournament and someone keeps saying "we should go watch one somewhere," Monday is the day. Argentina in the World Cup is not something you pencil in for next time.
scoring_notes: World Cup matches involving Messi's Argentina carry massive cultural weight right now. This is a one-time match, not a recurring event, which keeps the uniqueness score strong. East Cobb's 42-year-old median skews toward people who have been watching Messi for 20 years. Free outdoor setting with great food access is highly accessible.

event_name: Father's Day Brunch at C. Ellet's Steakhouse
blurb:
Father's Day falling on a Braves game day is honestly the best possible outcome, and C. Ellet's is leaning all the way into it.

Show up before the 1:35 PM first pitch for jazz brunch, mimosas for the table, and a menu that doesn't mess around. We're talking Center Cut Filet, Bone-In Ribeye for Two, and the kind of brunch that actually fills you up before you walk over to the park. The Battery is right there. You eat well, you watch the game. Dad wins.

If you've been trying to figure out how to make Father's Day feel like more than just a card and a handshake, this is the move. Reservations will go fast on a game day Sunday, so don't sit on it.
scoring_notes: This audience has the income for a steakhouse brunch without blinking. Father's Day timing is a natural hook. The combination of a special occasion meal plus a Braves game is a uniquely compelling package for East Cobb families with teens or adult kids. Strong adult skew, which fits the mix of established families and empty nesters.

blurb:
If you've ever sat in the stands at Truist Park and wondered what it looks like from the press box, or what the dugout feels like when the crowd is gone and the lights are low, this is your chance to find out.

Moonlight Tours take you behind the velvet rope at one of the newest ballparks in baseball. You start at the Blue Moon Brewery, cold beer in hand, then get a guided walk through the places fans never go: the press box, the dugout, the kind of access that usually requires a credential hanging around your neck. The moon and stars over the outfield are just the backdrop.

This is a Thursday night that actually feels like an event. Not a game, not a tailgate. Something different. Grab someone you want to impress and get your tickets before they're gone.
scoring_notes: East Cobb skews heavily Braves country. Behind-the-scenes access at Truist Park is a genuine can't-miss experience that money alone can't buy at a regular game. Adults and older teens will love it. Strong fit for established families and empty nesters alike. Thursday night timing works well for this income bracket.

blurb: Madison Beer is playing the Roxy on July 1, which lands on a Wednesday but right at the start of a holiday week when a lot of people have already mentally clocked out. The Roxy is one of the better mid-size venues in Atlanta, good sound, good sight lines, and the Battery around it makes for a solid pre-show dinner. Beer has built a real following in the 20-something demographic and the Roxy crowd for this one will have energy. If you've got someone in your household who's been waiting for this tour to come through Atlanta, now is the time to actually buy the tickets instead of waiting and paying double on the secondary market.
scoring_notes: Madison Beer skews younger than the median Perimeter Post reader at 38, but the mix of young professionals in this audience means there's a real segment here. The Roxy is a great venue and the Battery location adds value. Scored lower on demographic fit because the artist's core audience is late teens to mid-20s, which is on the younger end of this newsletter's readership. Still worth including as a pick for the younger professional segment.

blurb: Sewell Mill's MiniCon is back for 2026 and it's exactly what it sounds like: one day, every fandom, no gatekeeping. Games, vendors, food trucks, and workshops fill the cultural center from 10 to 5, and the vibe skews fun and accessible over intimidating and niche. If you've got kids who are into gaming, anime, comics, or really anything with a fan base, this is a Saturday well spent. And honestly, if you're an adult who grew up on any of this stuff, there's no shame in showing up solo either. It's free, it's local, and it's the kind of day that's hard to have a bad time at.
scoring_notes: The young families and younger end of the professional demographic in this audience will find this genuinely useful. It's free, locally produced, and the all-ages format gives it broad reach. The uniqueness score is moderate because fan conventions exist in various forms around Atlanta, but a free, neighborhood-scale version at Sewell Mill is more accessible and less overwhelming than Dragon Con. Good Saturday option.

event_name: Roswell Music Club: Cameron King, Tenor
blurb: Cameron King is one of classical music's most talked-about young tenors right now, and he's performing at Roswell River Landing on Sunday the 21st as part of the Roswell Music Club series. He trained at Indiana University's Jacobs School of Music and has been racking up performance credits that put him well beyond the local circuit. The River Landing is a beautiful outdoor setting on the Chattahoochee, which makes this the rare classical music event that doesn't require a dress code or a serious commitment to the art form. It's a Sunday, the setting does half the work for you, and you'll walk away having heard something genuinely impressive. Keep an eye on the Roswell Music Club page for time and ticket details.
scoring_notes: The highly educated, higher-income Perimeter audience has real overlap with classical and live music fans who want something more elevated than a cover band. The River Landing outdoor setting softens the barrier to entry for people who aren't die-hard classical listeners. Cameron King as a rising star adds a discovery angle, meaning this audience gets to say they saw him early. Time not confirmed is a flag, but the event itself is real and the fit is solid.

event_name: Atlanta Braves vs. St. Louis Cardinals
blurb: The Cardinals are in town for a series at Truist Park, and June 30 is a Tuesday night that actually has a good reason to get off the couch. Truist is one of the better ballpark experiences in the country, the Battery makes the whole area worth showing up early for, and a midweek Braves game in late June hits differently than a packed weekend series. Tickets are easy to find, you won't be fighting half of Atlanta for a parking spot, and the weather in the evenings has finally gotten to a point where sitting outside for three hours isn't a commitment. Grab a group, get there by 6, and do dinner at the Battery before first pitch.
scoring_notes: Braves games are a staple for this audience demographic and Truist Park is close enough to feel local. The Cardinals are a genuine rivalry matchup with national fan base crossover, which adds a small bump to the uniqueness score. Midweek timing is a mild disadvantage but also means better parking, better access, and a more relaxed crowd, which this audience tends to appreciate. The Battery dining scene makes it a full evening rather than just a game.

event_name: National Typewriter Day with Typewriter Tom
venue: Mimms Museum of Technology and Art, Roswell
blurb: National Typewriter Day is June 20, and Typewriter Tom is bringing his collection of iconic machines back to the Mimms Museum for an afternoon called "The Machines That Wrote America." This is not your average Saturday museum visit. Tom has done this before and the collection is genuinely impressive, and the story of how typewriters shaped journalism, literature, and American culture is more interesting than you'd expect. If you've got a soft spot for analog things, a curious kid who has never seen one up close, or a parent who actually used these machines at work, this is a low-key great afternoon. It's in Roswell, it runs until 5, and it's the one thing on your weekend agenda that nobody else will have done.
scoring_notes: The 70% college-educated audience skews toward cultural and intellectually interesting events, which is exactly what this is. The Mimms Museum is small and under the radar, which makes it feel like a genuine discovery rather than a programmed outing. Uniqueness score is high because there is genuinely nowhere else in the region doing this on this date. Family-friendly but also works for the empty nester segment that wants something different on a Saturday afternoon.

event_name: Argentina vs. Austria World Cup Watch Party at Campus 244
venue: The Lawn at Campus 244, 244 Perimeter Center Pkwy NE, Dunwoody
blurb: Argentina, the defending World Cup champions, play Austria on Monday, and Campus 244 is putting a free watch party on the lawn for it. No tickets, no reservation, just show up and watch Messi and company do their thing in the middle of your lunch hour. The Perimeter Center location makes this genuinely convenient if you're working nearby or just want a midday excuse to be outside. It's casual, it's free, and Argentina vs. Austria is not a throwaway group stage match. If you've been looking for an easy way to plug into the World Cup energy this summer without committing to a full night out, this is it.
scoring_notes: Campus 244 sits directly in the Perimeter audience's backyard, which is a strong geographic fit. Free entry, outdoor setting, and a marquee World Cup matchup make this easy to recommend. The young professional segment of this audience is very likely to follow international soccer. Midday Monday timing is the one soft spot but it's summer, schedules are looser, and the convenience of the location offsets it.

event_name: World Cup Weekend Watch Party at Zanzibar ATL
venue: Zanzibar ATL, 350 Northridge Rd, Sandy Springs
blurb: Atlanta is one of the host cities for the 2026 World Cup, and the energy around it this summer is real. Zanzibar is leaning all the way in with an elevated watch party experience that pairs live soccer with brunch vibes, after-party energy, and the kind of crowd that actually knows what they're watching. This isn't a bar with a game on in the background. It's a full production, and it lands on June 19, which means you've got a Friday night and a reason to go out already built in. Sandy Springs is right in your backyard, so there's no excuse not to check it out. Get there, find a seat, and enjoy the fact that the world just showed up in your city.
scoring_notes: The 2026 World Cup is a once-every-four-years event and Atlanta hosting makes local watch parties genuinely special rather than just routine bar sports nights. The Zanzibar venue skews adult and upscale, which matches the young professional and empty nester mix here. Friday evening timing is ideal. The $105K median income audience won't blink at a cover or drink minimums.
